name: S3 Data Lakes for Machine Learning
description: Designing S3-based data lake architectures for storing, organizing, and accessing ML training data at scale
key_facts: S3 supports Parquet, ORC, and CSV formats; columnar formats like Parquet reduce I/O for feature-heavy ML datasets
S3 Select and Glacier Select enable querying subsets of data without downloading entire objects
Lake Formation provides fine-grained column and row-level access control for data governance
Partitioning data by date, category, or other dimensions optimizes query performance and SageMaker data access
S3 Lifecycle policies automatically tier infrequently accessed training data to lower-cost storage classes
common_misconceptions: CSV is the best format for ML training data (Parquet provides better compression and columnar access for large feature sets)
All S3 storage classes have the same retrieval latency (Glacier and Deep Archive have retrieval delays unsuitable for active training)

name: AWS Glue ETL for Feature Engineering
description: Using AWS Glue for extract, transform, and load operations to prepare and engineer features for ML models
key_facts: Glue crawlers automatically infer schema from S3, RDS, and other data sources and populate the Data Catalog
Glue ETL jobs run Apache Spark for distributed data transformation at scale with built-in ML transforms like FindMatches
DynamicFrames provide schema flexibility for semi-structured data without requiring a fixed schema upfront
Glue DataBrew provides visual data preparation for cleaning, normalizing, and transforming data without code
Job bookmarks track processed data to support incremental ETL and avoid reprocessing
common_misconceptions: Glue ETL is only for batch processing (Glue also supports streaming ETL with micro-batches from Kinesis and Kafka)
Glue requires a fixed schema before processing (DynamicFrames handle schema evolution and semi-structured data)

name: Kinesis for Streaming ML Data
description: Ingesting and processing real-time streaming data with Kinesis for online ML inference and feature computation
key_facts: Kinesis Data Streams provides real-time data ingestion with configurable shard-based throughput (1 MB/s write, 2 MB/s read per shard)
Kinesis Data Firehose delivers data to S3, Redshift, or OpenSearch with optional Lambda transformations for near-real-time loading
Kinesis Data Analytics uses Apache Flink for real-time feature computation and anomaly detection on streaming data
Random Cut Forest algorithm in Kinesis Data Analytics detects anomalies in streaming time-series data
Enhanced fan-out provides dedicated throughput per consumer for low-latency stream processing
common_misconceptions: Kinesis Data Firehose and Kinesis Data Streams are the same service (Streams provides real-time ingestion with custom consumers; Firehose provides managed delivery to destinations)
Kinesis retains data indefinitely (default retention is 24 hours, extendable up to 365 days with additional cost)

name: SageMaker Notebooks and Data Exploration
description: Using SageMaker notebook instances and Studio for interactive data exploration, visualization, and analysis
key_facts: SageMaker Studio provides an integrated IDE with notebooks, experiments, model registry, and debugger in a single interface
Notebook instances support direct access to S3 data and integration with Glue Data Catalog for schema discovery
SageMaker Data Wrangler provides a visual interface for data exploration, transformation, and feature engineering
Lifecycle configurations automate notebook instance setup tasks like installing libraries and mounting EFS volumes
SageMaker Processing jobs run data analysis workloads on managed infrastructure separate from notebook instances
common_misconceptions: Notebooks should be used for production training (notebooks are for exploration; use SageMaker Training jobs or Pipelines for production workloads)
SageMaker Studio and notebook instances are the same (Studio is a full IDE with additional features; notebook instances are standalone Jupyter servers)

name: Feature Selection and Engineering Techniques
description: Methods for selecting, creating, and transforming features to improve ML model performance
key_facts: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) reduces dimensionality by projecting features onto orthogonal components that capture maximum variance
Feature importance from tree-based models (XGBoost, Random Forest) ranks features by their contribution to predictions
One-hot encoding converts categorical variables to binary vectors; target encoding maps categories to target statistics for high-cardinality features
Handling missing values with imputation (mean, median, mode, or KNN-based) preserves training data volume versus dropping rows
Feature scaling (standardization, min-max normalization) is critical for distance-based algorithms but not required for tree-based models
common_misconceptions: More features always improve model accuracy (too many features can cause overfitting and the curse of dimensionality)
Tree-based models require feature scaling (decision trees and gradient boosting are invariant to monotonic transformations of features)

name: Data Distributions and Visualization
description: Analyzing data distributions, detecting outliers, understanding class imbalance, and creating visualizations for ML insights
key_facts: Histograms and box plots reveal distribution shape, central tendency, spread, and outliers in feature data
Class imbalance in classification problems can be addressed with SMOTE oversampling, undersampling, or weighted loss functions
Correlation matrices identify multicollinearity between features that may cause instability in linear models
Scatter plots and pair plots visualize relationships between features and the target variable
Amazon SageMaker Clarify detects bias in training data and provides pre-training and post-training bias metrics
common_misconceptions: Class imbalance can always be fixed by oversampling the minority class (oversampling can lead to overfitting; a combination of techniques and appropriate evaluation metrics is better)
Outliers should always be removed (outliers may represent valid edge cases that the model should learn; domain knowledge determines the appropriate treatment)

name: Data Visualization and QuickSight for ML
description: Using visualization tools and Amazon QuickSight to communicate ML data insights and model results
key_facts: QuickSight ML Insights provides anomaly detection, forecasting, and auto-narrative capabilities built into dashboards
SPICE engine in QuickSight provides in-memory query acceleration for large datasets with automatic refresh
Confusion matrices visualize classification model performance showing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives
ROC curves plot the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate across classification thresholds
Residual plots for regression models reveal non-linear patterns that the model fails to capture
common_misconceptions: QuickSight replaces SageMaker for ML (QuickSight provides embedded ML insights for business users but does not replace full ML model development)
A single metric like accuracy is sufficient to evaluate model performance (accuracy is misleading for imbalanced datasets; use precision, recall, F1, and AUC together)

name: XGBoost on SageMaker
description: Training and tuning XGBoost gradient boosted tree models using SageMaker's built-in algorithm for classification and regression
key_facts: XGBoost supports classification (binary:logistic, multi:softmax) and regression (reg:squarederror, reg:linear) objectives
Key hyperparameters: max_depth controls tree complexity, eta (learning rate) controls step size, num_round sets boosting iterations
Regularization parameters alpha (L1) and lambda (L2) prevent overfitting by penalizing model complexity
SageMaker XGBoost accepts CSV and libsvm input formats; data must be numeric with the target in the first column for CSV
Distributed training across multiple instances is supported for large datasets via data parallelism
common_misconceptions: XGBoost requires feature scaling (tree-based models split on feature values and are invariant to monotonic scaling)
Increasing num_round always improves performance (too many rounds causes overfitting; early stopping should be used to find the optimal number)

name: Linear Learner and Built-in Algorithms
description: Using SageMaker Linear Learner for linear classification and regression, and understanding when to apply various built-in algorithms
key_facts: Linear Learner supports binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression with automatic model tuning
Requires numeric features and benefits from feature scaling; can normalize data automatically during training
Supports L1 and L2 regularization to prevent overfitting and encourage sparse feature selection
Input format is recordIO-protobuf for best performance or CSV; training data must be shuffled
SageMaker also provides built-in algorithms for KNN, factorization machines, random cut forest, and image classification
common_misconceptions: Linear Learner can only do regression (it supports binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression)
Built-in algorithms cannot compete with custom deep learning models (for tabular data with clear feature engineering, built-in algorithms often outperform complex models)

name: SageMaker Training Jobs and Infrastructure
description: Configuring and optimizing SageMaker training jobs including instance selection, distributed training, and managed spot training
key_facts: Training jobs provision compute instances, download data from S3, run the training algorithm, and save model artifacts back to S3
Pipe mode streams data directly from S3 to the training container, reducing startup time and disk requirements versus File mode
Managed spot training uses EC2 Spot Instances for up to 90% cost savings with automatic checkpointing for fault tolerance
SageMaker Debugger captures training metrics and tensors in real-time to detect issues like vanishing gradients and overfitting
Distributed training strategies include data parallelism (split data across instances) and model parallelism (split model across GPUs)
common_misconceptions: Larger instance types always train faster (training speed depends on data loading, algorithm parallelization, and GPU utilization; I/O can be the bottleneck)
Managed spot training will always complete (spot instances can be interrupted; checkpointing is essential for fault tolerance)

name: Hyperparameter Tuning and Optimization
description: Using SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning to find optimal hyperparameters through Bayesian optimization, random search, and grid search strategies
key_facts: Bayesian optimization uses previous training results to guide the search for optimal hyperparameters more efficiently than random search
Objective metric defines what the tuning job optimizes (e.g., minimize validation:rmse or maximize validation:auc)
Warm start allows a new tuning job to build upon results from previous tuning jobs to converge faster
Hyperparameter ranges can be continuous, integer, or categorical; each type uses different search strategies
Maximum parallel training jobs trades off between speed and Bayesian optimization effectiveness (fewer parallel jobs = better optimization)
common_misconceptions: Random search is always inferior to Bayesian optimization (random search can be more effective in very high-dimensional hyperparameter spaces or with limited budget)
More tuning job iterations always lead to better results (diminishing returns occur; the objective metric improvements plateau after sufficient exploration)

name: Model Evaluation Metrics and Validation
description: Evaluating ML model performance using appropriate metrics, cross-validation, and understanding bias-variance trade-offs
key_facts: AUC-ROC measures overall classification performance independent of threshold; values above 0.8 indicate good discrimination
Precision measures the fraction of positive predictions that are actually positive; important when false positives are costly
Recall (sensitivity) measures the fraction of actual positives correctly identified; important when false negatives are costly
RMSE and MAE evaluate regression models; RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily than MAE
K-fold cross-validation reduces overfitting risk by training on different data splits and averaging performance metrics
common_misconceptions: High training accuracy means the model will generalize well (large gap between training and validation accuracy indicates overfitting)
Accuracy is the best metric for all classification problems (for imbalanced datasets, precision, recall, F1, and AUC are more informative)

name: SageMaker Endpoints and Deployment Patterns
description: Deploying ML models to SageMaker real-time endpoints, batch transform, and serverless inference with A/B testing strategies
key_facts: Real-time endpoints provide persistent HTTPS endpoints for synchronous inference with auto-scaling based on invocations or CPU
Production variants enable A/B testing by routing traffic percentages to different model versions on the same endpoint
Batch Transform processes large datasets offline without maintaining a persistent endpoint, ideal for periodic bulk predictions
Serverless inference automatically scales to zero when idle, eliminating costs during periods of no traffic
Multi-model endpoints host multiple models on a single endpoint instance, dynamically loading models from S3 on demand
common_misconceptions: Real-time endpoints are the only deployment option (SageMaker offers batch transform, serverless inference, and asynchronous inference for different use cases)
A/B testing requires separate endpoints (production variants on a single endpoint can split traffic between model versions)

name: SageMaker Model Monitor
description: Monitoring deployed ML models for data quality drift, model quality degradation, bias drift, and feature attribution changes
key_facts: Data quality monitoring detects schema violations and statistical drift in input features compared to a baseline
Model quality monitoring tracks prediction accuracy by comparing predictions with ground truth labels
Bias drift monitoring uses SageMaker Clarify to detect changes in bias metrics over time on deployed models
Feature attribution drift monitors SHAP values to detect when feature importance changes compared to training baseline
Monitoring schedules run on configurable intervals and publish results to S3 with CloudWatch alerts for violations
common_misconceptions: Model Monitor automatically retrains models when drift is detected (it alerts on drift; retraining requires a separate automated pipeline)
Data quality drift always means model performance has degraded (data distribution changes may not affect predictions if the model is robust to those specific changes)

name: SageMaker Pipelines and MLOps
description: Building automated ML workflows with SageMaker Pipelines, Model Registry, and CI/CD patterns for production ML systems
key_facts: SageMaker Pipelines defines ML workflows as DAGs with steps for processing, training, evaluation, and deployment
Model Registry stores model versions with approval status, metadata, and lineage for governed model promotion
Condition steps enable automated decision-making in pipelines (e.g., deploy only if accuracy exceeds a threshold)
Pipeline parameters allow reuse of the same pipeline definition with different hyperparameters or data sources
Integration with CodePipeline and CodeBuild enables CI/CD automation for model retraining and deployment
common_misconceptions: SageMaker Pipelines replaces CI/CD tools like CodePipeline (Pipelines handles ML workflow orchestration; CI/CD tools handle code deployment and are complementary)
Models in the Model Registry are automatically deployed to production (models must be approved and a deployment step must be triggered)
